coredata.dc:title: Facile fabrication of Ag2(bdc)@Ag nano-composites with strong green emission and their response to sulfide anion in aqueous medium
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Yiwei
Wu, Xuemin
Niu, Baitong
Zeng, Yanping
Zhu, Meihua
Guo, Hongxu
coredata.dc:description: A novel composites constructed MOF(s) and Ag nanoclusters, Ag2(bdc)@Ag (H2bdc is 1, 4-benzenedicarboxylate) have been simply fabricated through solution strategy for the first time, and characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), X-ray diffraction (XRD), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy(XPS). The as-prepared composites were found to be an effective material for the selective fluorescence detection of microgram levels of S2− in aqueous solution based on fluorescence quenching effects. Under optimized experimental conditions, the probe exhibited a stable response for S2– over a concentration range from 0.2μM to 30μM and a detection limit of 0.21μM. The feasibility of the proposed method was further demonstrated through the detection of S2– in real samples, with the satisfactory recoveries in the range of 97.8–101.9%. The possible sensing quenching mechanism for the detection of S2– could be attributed to the formation of Ag2S during the addition of S2– into the Ag2(bdc)@Ag dispersion system. The proposed method for the detection of S2– has potential application in the environmental pollutant analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ag2(bdc)@Ag
Nanocluster composites
Fluorescence probe
Sulfide anion
Facile fabrication

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst pubtype ref vitae

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province, China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province, China (Grant No. 2017J01420 ). Yiwei Wu received his bachelor’s degree in 2017 and is currently a postgraduate College of Chemistry and Environment, Minnan Normal University, Zhangzhou, China, researching electronic devices and chemical sensors. Xuemin Wu is a grade three undergraduate in College of Chemistry and Environment, Minnan Normal University, Zhangzhou, China. Baitong Niu is a grade three undergraduate in College of Chemistry and Environment, Minnan Normal University, Zhangzhou, China. Yanping Zeng is a grade four undergraduate in College of Chemistry and Environment, Minnan Normal University, Zhangzhou, China. Meihua Zhu received her bachelor’s degree in 2016 and is currently a postgraduate College of Chemistry and Environment, Minnan Normal University, Zhangzhou, China, researching electronic devices and chemical sensors. Hongxu Guo was born in Fujian, P. R. China in 1972. He received his PhD degree in physical chemistry from college of chemistry, Fuzhou University (2006), P. R. China. He is a professor in College of Chemistry & Environment, Minnan Normal University, Zhangzhou, China. His current interests are mainly focused on facile green fabrication of the nanostructured metal–organic frameworks and their application in electronic devices and chemical sensors.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD spectra of as-prepared Ag2(bdc)@Ag composites, and simulated Ag2(bdc) MOFs and Ag.
SEM (a) and TEM (b) images of as-prepared Ag2(bdc)@Ag composites.
XPS spectra of as-prepared Ag2(bdc)@Ag composites.
The dispersibility in aqueous solution for the as-prepared sample.

Fluorescence spectra (λex =210nm) of (a) free Ag2(bdc)@Ag (1) and (b) Ag2(bdc)@Ag in the presence of S2− anions; inset: photographs of Ag2(bdc)@Ag and Ag2(bdc)@Ag in the presence of S2− anions under UV light (254nm). The concentrations of S2− ions and Ag2(bdc)@Ag are 100μM and 0.1mgmL−1, respectively.

(a) The effect of the pH on the fluorescence intensity of Ag2(bdc)@Ag before and after addition of sulfide anions; (b) the effect of the time on the fluorescence intensity of Ag2(bdc)@Ag after addition of sulfide anions.

Fluorescence spectra (a) and bar graph (b) of probe 1 in the presence of [1] and other common anions. [1]=5.0×10−6 M, [A n
−]=5.0×10−4 M. A n
− include I−, F−, CO3 2−, N3 −, SO4 2−, Br−, Cl−, SO3 2−, CH3COO−, PO4 3− and C2O4 2−.

Fluorescence intensity changes of the Ag2(bdc)@Ag to sulfide anions in the presence of various test anions in PBS water (pH 7). Key: (a) S2−; (b) S2− +C2O4 2−; (c) S2− +I−; (d) S2− +F−; (e) S2− +CO3 2−; (f) S2− +N3 −; (g) S2− +SO3 2−; (h) S2− +Cl−; (i) S2− +SO4 2−; (j) S2− +Br−; (k) S2− +CH3COO−; and (l) S2− +PO4 3−.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of Ag2(bdc)@Ag in the presence of various concentrations of S2−; (b) plot of fluorescence intensity decrease (Fo−F)/Fo versus the logarithm of concentration of S2− added (the error bar represents the standard deviation of three replicated measurements).
XRD patterns of the Ag2S obtained from Ag2(bdc)@Ag and simulated pattern (JCPDS no. 14-0072).
Comparision of different probes for determination of sulfide anions.
Results of recovery studies from tap water samples spiked with S2−.

head.ce:title: Facile fabrication of Ag2(bdc)@Ag nano-composites with strong green emission and their response to sulfide anion in aqueous medium

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Environment, Minnan Normal University, Zhangzhou 363000, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel composites constructed MOF(s) and Ag nanoclusters, Ag2(bdc)@Ag (H2bdc is 1, 4-benzenedicarboxylate) have been simply fabricated through solution strategy for the first time, and characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), X-ray diffraction (XRD), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy(XPS). The as-prepared composites were found to be an effective material for the selective fluorescence detection of microgram levels of S2− in aqueous solution based on fluorescence quenching effects. Under optimized experimental conditions, the probe exhibited a stable response for S2– over a concentration range from 0.2μM to 30μM and a detection limit of 0.21μM. The feasibility of the proposed method was further demonstrated through the detection of S2– in real samples, with the satisfactory recoveries in the range of 97.8–101.9%. The possible sensing quenching mechanism for the detection of S2– could be attributed to the formation of Ag2S during the addition of S2– into the Ag2(bdc)@Ag dispersion system. The proposed method for the detection of S2– has potential application in the environmental pollutant analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: With the continuous development of society and economy, degree of industrialization becomes increasingly higher and so does requirements on environmental monitoring and management. In this way, the refuse with sulfur compound has become one of the main pollutants, especially in fields like papermaking, petroleum cracking, printing & dyeing, process hides, electroplate and coking. The maximum allowable concentration of sulfide in wastewater stipulated in National Standard GB8978-88 is 1.0mg/L. The sulfide in waterbody has a stronger chemical action on numerous metal ions necessary for living organisms and a stronger corrosion on plant roots and stems. For this reason, only 0.07μg/L of sulfide concentration in drinking water may also have an effect on the water taste. Hence, measuring sulfide contents in the pollutants under the environmental monitoring may act as a vital index.

At present, the commonly adopted methods for the sulfide measurement include capillary electrophoresis [1], fiber optical sensor [2], and gas chromatography [3], etc. All these analytical methods request for the complex instruments, highly demanded operation techniques or multifarious analytical procedures. Therefore, developing a simple and practicable sulfide analytical method with low determination cost and good selectivity may have an important application merit. Due to its high selectivity and sensitivity, fluorescence analysis has become an all-important and effect spectrochemistry analysis method [4–7].

Metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) have recently emerged as promising materials for gas storage/separation, chemical sensing, catalysis, biomedicine, magnetism, clean energy technology and optoelectronics [8–12]. Especially, as fluorescence-based chemical sensors, MOFs offer great promise by virtue of their crystalline nature, permanent porosity, designable/modifiable pores, systematically tunable band gaps and electronic structures [13–15]. To the best of our knowledge, the detection with high selectivity and sensitivity for sulfide anion in solution over MOFs-based fluorescence probe has been seldom reported at present. On the other hand, as a kind of novel fluorescent nano-material, the metallic nanocluster has aroused more and more attention in the application of fluorescent sensor due to its small size, stability, higher quantum yield and low toxicity[16–18]. In order to explore nano-composites constructed from MOFs and metallic nanocluster in the application of fluorescent probe, a novel composites forming MOF(Ag) and Ag nanoclusters, that is Ag2(bdc)@Ag, was fabricated for the first time through simple solution method and then established a recognition and detection for S2− by the novel fluorescent sensor based on its strong fluorescence signal and obvious quenching effect on S2− in solution. Under the optimum experimental condition, S2− within 0.2−30μM has a good linear response to the fluorescence quenching efficiency with and a detection limit of 0.21μM. The proposed method for the detection of S2– has potential application in the environmental pollutant analysis.

1, 4-benzenedicarboxylate (H2bdc, C6H4(COOH)2), silver nitrate (Ag(NO3)) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co. LLC (USA). Double deionized water was obtained with a Pall Cascada laboratory water system. All other chemicals were of at least analytical grade and used without any purification.

An aqueous solution of H2bdc was prepared by dissolving 0.16g of TPA in 10mL 1:1 DMF-water mixture, and metal ion solutions with 0.16g (1mmol) of AgNO3 was prepared in 10mL double deionized water. The prepared metal ion solution was transferred dropwise into the TPA solution in a beaker under constant stirring for 10min. Black brown colored precipitate was observed upon complexing of TPA with Ag+ ion and mixing continued for 20min under 85°C, the resulting product was filtered and washed with ethanol and water several times. The product was dried finally at 353K overnight.

Na2S was used as the S2− source during the sensitivity studies. A S2− stock solution (0.1M) was prepared, and various concentrations were obtained by serial dilution of the stock solution. 0.1g/L Ag2(bdc)@Ag fluorescent sensor solution, phosphate buffer saline (PBS) solution with pH=6.86, and 1×10−2 M stock solutions for other anions (I−, F−, CO3 2−, N3 −, SO4 2−, Br−, Cl−, SO3 2−, Ac−, PO4 3−, C2O4 2−, all anionic solutions were prepared by the relevant sodium salt or potassium) were prepared, respectively. To detect the S2−, 5mL of different concentrations S2− were added to 8mL of the obtained Ag2(bdc)@Ag fluorescent probe solution and diluted to 50L with PBS buffer solution, and then the mixtures were incubated at 28°C for 80min before measuring the fluorescence spectra. The fluorescence intensity (I) of the mixed system and fluorescence intensity (I0) of the blank solution at the maximum emission wavelength were measured, respectively.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of the sample was recorded using a Bruker D8 diffractmeter in Cu Kα-ray target (λ=0.1542nm) at a target voltage of 40kV, target current 40mA, scanning range of 2 θ of 5°–80°, and scan rate of 6°min−1. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were obtained using a scanning electron microscope (S-4800) at an accelerating voltage of 5kV. TEM images were obtained on a transmission electron microscope (Tecnai G2 F20, FEI compony) equipped with an energy dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDAX). XPS experiments were carried out in an ultrahigh vacuum using the ESCALAB Mark II X−ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, VG Scientific, UK) with Mg Kα radiation (1253.6eV) from the Mg anode source. The high-resolution scans of core level spectra were recorded with an energy step of 0.05eV and set to 15eV pass energy. The binding energy was referenced to at 284.6eV to the C1s peak. Experimental data were deconvolved by Gaussian–Lorentzian mixture peak–fitting software.

Fig. 1 shows the XRD patterns of as-prepared Ag2(bdc)@Ag composites, simulated Ag2(bdc) complex and Ag. From the as-prepared Ag2(bdc)@Ag composites, the peaks at 10.6, 13.2, 16.5, 18.1, 23.9, 25, 30.9, 32.6, 33.8, 34.1, and 40.8° are assigned to diffractions of the complex Ag2(bdc) (CCDC No. 198096) [19]. The four additional peaks at 38.2, 44.5, 64.5 and 77.7° can be indexed respectively to (111), (200), (222) and (014) planes of face-centered-cubic metal Ag (JCPDS card no. 04-0783). Those results indicated that the composites contain two material compositions, compound Ag2(bdc) and metal Ag.

The morphology and microstructure of the as-prepared Ag2(bdc)@Ag powders were examined by SEM and TEM, as shown in Fig. 2 . The SEM image (Fig. 2a) reveals that the morphology of Ag2(bdc)@Ag consists of irregular blocks with some spherical particles covered on surface. The high resolution TEM of product clearly indicates that these blocks were made up of 4–5nm Ag nano-cluster and 50nm spherical MOFs(Ag) nanoparticles covered on the surface were reunited by some Ag2(bdc)@Ag composites.

In order to determine the chemical composition of Ag2(bdc)@Ag composites and further identify the chemical state of the Ag element in the sample, XPS characterization was performed as shown in Fig. 3 . In the XPS experiment, the samples are exposed to monochromic X-radiation, and the properties of inner-shell electrons are to be probed. According to the XPS survey spectrum of Ag2(bdc)@Ag sample (Fig. 3(a)), three kinds of elements, that is Ag, O, and C were detected in the sample, in which the peaks at 573, 604, 1130eV can be attributed to characteristic peak of Ag 3p3/2, Ag 3p1/2, Ag(A), respectively.

Fig. 3(b) displays the Ag 3d level spectrum. The Ag 3d3/2 and Ag 3d5/2 spin–orbital photoelectrons were located at binding energies of 368.3eV and 374.4eV, respectively. According to the previous report [20,21], the peaks at 368.3 and 374.4eV were attributed to metallic silver, and these two peaks with a spin energy separation of 6.1eV further indicated that the Ag0 existed in the Ag2(bdc)@Ag composite. The C1s emission spectrum can be divided into three main peaks, as shown in Fig. 3(c). The binding energy in 284.8eV was obtained in the XPS analysis is standardized for specimen charging using C 1s as the reference. The peak in 289.4eV was attributed from the carboxylate carbon in OCOH, while the peaks in 288.3eV are associated with the aryl carbon from benzene ring. By using a Gaussian fitting method, the O 1s emission spectrum (Fig. 3(d)) can be divided into three main peaks. The component at 531.5eV is typical of metal-oxygen (AgO) bonds, and 532.3eV perhaps comes from OCO bonds in the organic ligand, while the peak 533.6eV may be attributed from the OH groups from the adsorbed H2O molecules.

The formation of Ag nanoclusters in the simple solution strategy is interesting. According to the previous literature [22,23], we can conclude that the Ag species are derived from the reduction reaction of the Ag+ with the aid of DMF solution. DMF has been demonstrated to be an effective reducing agent for the preparation of metal nanoparticles with tunable shape and size under suitable conditions. It can reduce Ag+ to form Ag nanoparticles even at room temperature, and the reduction process involved in the reaction is:
HCONMe2 +2Ag+ +H2O→2Ag0 +Me2NCOOH+2H+

The dispersibility of the as-prepared Ag2(bdc)@Ag composites in solution is very important for fluorescent material. The Ag2(bdc)@Ag composites of 0.1g/L in solution were dispersedly stood for 0h, 3h, 7h and 12h with subsidence situations recorded by camera. The images of the dispersibility are shown in Fig. 4 , showing that the fluorescent sensor was not subsided significantly within 12h, which indicated that the good dispensability of Ag2(bdc)@Ag composites in aqueous solution was observed. Thus result is conductive for the follow-up experiments.

Upon titration of S2− ions into the Ag2(bdc)@Ag solution, there appeared some phenomena: (1) a quenching process could be observed by the naked-eye under a UV light source with a wavelength of 254nm (inset in Fig. 5 ); (2) when the solution was measured with a fluorescence spectrometer with excitation at 210nm, the peak at 490nm in the emission spectra of Ag2(bdc)@Ag obviously (curves a and b in Fig. 5). These results indicated that S2− has an obvious quenching effect on the Ag2(bdc)@Ag solution.

It is well known that the anions determination is pH dependent [24]. In order to find the optimum conditions for the determination of sulfide ion, the influence of various pH on the luminescent of Ag2(bdc)@Ag was investigated in the range of 7–11. Aqueous phosphate buffer solutions were used to achieve the desired pHs. Fig. 6 (a) shows the effect of the pH on the fluorescence intensity of Ag2(bdc)@Ag before and after addition of sulfide ion. The results obtained from this study showed that the luminescence intensity of Ag2(bdc)@Ag in the absence of S2− gradually decreased with increase of pH, while that of Ag2(bdc)@Ag in the presence of S2− increased with increase of pH. With a view to the maximum downing region for fluorescence intensity of Ag2(bdc)@Ag before and after addition of sulfide ion, an optimum pH of 7 was selected for next studies.

In order to validate the reaction time, the fluorescence intensity of a typical mixing of Ag2(bdc)@Ag and sulfide solution was also studied at maximum emission wavelength (λex =210nm, λem =490nm), under optimum conditions. As can be seen from Fig. 6(b), the fluorescence intensity of the solution system for mixing of Ag2(bdc)@Ag and sulfide solution was rapidly reduced from 540 to 350 at the moment S2− was added, then gradually reduced for the increase of time afterwards and basically kept in an approximate stable status at 4800s. Hence, 4800s was selected as the optimal time for detection of S2− over Ag2(bdc)@Ag for next studies.

Target compound Ag2(bdc)@Ag is water soluble, which makes it useful for practical applications. The fluorescence spectra were obtained by excitation of the fluorophore at 290nm in 10mM PBS 100% water solution at pH=7, and a strong emission peak was observed at 490nm. First, to obtain insight into the sensing properties of Ag2(bdc)@Ag, the emission was examined with various normal anions. As shown in Fig. 7 , the addition of 5 equiv of I−, F−, CO3 2−, N3 −, SO4 2−, Br−, Cl−, SO3 2−, CH3COO−, PO4 3− and C2O4 2− had no obvious effect on the fluorescence emission. When 1 equiv of S2− was added to the solution of Ag2(bdc)@Ag, dramatic fluorescent quenching (quenching efficiency (Io −I)/Io ×100=86%) was observed, suggesting that compound Ag2(bdc)@Ag shows a specific response to S2− anions with good selectivity in solution.

In addition, whether the Ag2(bdc)@Ag nanocomposites could still retain the sensing response to sulfide anions under the potential interfering anions is very important for a fluorescent sensor. Thus, competition experiments of the Ag2(bdc)@Ag were further studied. As shown in Fig. 8 , the ensemble (2μM) was treated with sulfide anions (20 equiv) in the presence of various test anions (100 equiv). All of the relevant anions tested have virtually no influence on the fluorescence detection of sulfide anions. Thus, the system to be useful for high selectively sensing sulfide even involving these relevant anions.

Under the above optimum conditions, we carried out the fluorescence sensing experiment of Ag2(bdc)@Ag toward S2− anions in solution. As depicted in Fig. 9 (a), the Ag2(bdc)@Ag had a fluorescence emission peak at 490nm in the absence of S2−. With the increase of S2− concentration, the fluorescence intensity of Ag2(bdc)@Ag gradually decreased. Fig. 9(b) elucidates their corresponding calibration curve. There was a good linear dependence of descent rate of fluorescence intensity [(Fo−F)/Fo] on the logarithm of concentration of S2− added (log[S2]/μΜ, S2− concentration ranging from 0.2 to 30μM), wherein the equation of linear regression is y=0.2430+0.4048x and the correlation coefficient is R2 =0.9950. The relative standard deviation (RSD) was 1.95% for six replicate measurements, suggesting the good repeatability of this method. The detection limit of Ag2(bdc)@Ag at a signal-to-noise of 3 for S2− was 0.21μM, which was lower than the maximum level of S2− (15μM) in drinking water permitted by the World Health Organization (WHO) [25]. Compared with the published references for the detection for sulfide anions in aqueous medium [26–35], the present method shows better or comparable sensitivity and detection range (Table 1 ).

The sensing mechanism was also confirmed by XRD analysis. The fluorescence intensity of the Ag2(bdc)@Ag dispersion gradually reduced during the increase of S2− concentration. This is probably due to the formation of Ag2S precipitate from Ag2(bdc)@Ag and S2− in the solution. In order to validate this hypothesis, a simple experiment was carried out by the mixing-reaction experiments of Ag2(bdc)@Ag dispersion (20mL, 100μM) with Na2S solution (10mL, 2mM). The as-prepared dark-brown precipitate sample obtained after the mixing-reaction experiments mentioned above was then conducted by XDR pattern (see Fig. 10 ), which the XRD diffraction peaks were assigned to the Acanthite Ag2S (JCPDS no. 14-0072, P21/n, a =4.229, b =6.931, c =7.862Å). This result indicated that the reaction between Ag2(bdc)@Ag dispersion and S2− actually formed Ag2S precipitate and thus contributed to the fluorescence quenching.

The feasibility of Ag2(bdc)@Ag nanocomposites for detecting S2− in real samples was explored by using tap water. The water samples were spiked with standard solutions containing different concentrations of S2−. The results are summarized in Table 2 . The concentrations determined in spiked samples by the developed method were in good agreement with S2− spiked, along with satisfactory recoveries of 97.8–101.9%. The results demonstrated that the proposed method can act as a promising analytical method for monitoring S2− in real water samples.

In summary, a green-emitting Ag2(bdc)@Ag nanocomposites probe has been prepared by a simple and environmentally friendly approach. The fluorescence intensity of Ag2(bdc)@Ag nanocomposites was quenched significantly in the presence of S2− with static quenching properties. Based on the specific quenching of the fluorescence of Ag2(bdc)@Ag nanocomposites by S2−, we developed a sensitive and selective method for the determination of S2−. Compared with most known assay methods, the most outstanding advantage of the method is fairly economical and facile, not requiring expensive agents and complicated processes to obtain reliable detection results for real water samples. We expect this work can provide meaningful reference for the further design and application development of Ag2(bdc)@Ag nanocomposites toward cost-effective and simple orientation.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province, China (Grant No. 2017J01420).

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yiwei Wu received his bachelor’s degree in 2017 and is currently a postgraduate College of Chemistry and Environment, Minnan Normal University, Zhangzhou, China, researching electronic devices and chemical sensors.
Xuemin Wu is a grade three undergraduate in College of Chemistry and Environment, Minnan Normal University, Zhangzhou, China.
Baitong Niu is a grade three undergraduate in College of Chemistry and Environment, Minnan Normal University, Zhangzhou, China.
Yanping Zeng is a grade four undergraduate in College of Chemistry and Environment, Minnan Normal University, Zhangzhou, China.

Meihua Zhu received her bachelor’s degree in 2016 and is currently a postgraduate College of Chemistry and Environment, Minnan Normal University, Zhangzhou, China, researching electronic devices and chemical sensors.

Hongxu Guo was born in Fujian, P. R. China in 1972. He received his PhD degree in physical chemistry from college of chemistry, Fuzhou University (2006), P. R. China. He is a professor in College of Chemistry & Environment, Minnan Normal University, Zhangzhou, China. His current interests are mainly focused on facile green fabrication of the nanostructured metal–organic frameworks and their application in electronic devices and chemical sensors.
